original_abstract: Few behavioral phenomena are as ubiquitous as the speed-accuracy tradeoff (SAT). Observed across the animal kingdom—from insects and rodents to primates—the inverse relationship between decision speed and precision appears to be an inescapable property of choice behavior. Recently, the SAT has enjoyed a resurgence of interest as neuroscientific techniques begin to unravel its biological underpinnings and computational models increasingly treat it as a fundamental benchmark for decision-making. This paper provides a comprehensive overview of the SAT, beginning with a historical perspective on its role in shaping mathematical descriptions of cognitive processes. I then offer a "user’s guide" to SAT methodology, featuring a critical evaluation of common experimental manipulations and analytical techniques, alongside a discussion of the behavioral patterns that emerge when the tradeoff is directly controlled. Finally, I review the practical applications of these methodologies across several research domains.
humanized_abstract: The speed-accuracy tradeoff (SAT) is one of the most ubiquitous phenomena in behavioral science. It is observed in almost every species, from insects and rodents to primates. Recently, advances in neuroscience are starting to shed light on its neural substrates, and many computational models use the SAT as a gold standard for decision-making. Here, we give a historical account of the evolution of models of decision-making in the context of the SAT, and then provide a 'user’s guide' to studying the SAT, with a critical discussion of the different manipulations and techniques that have been employed to study this phenomenon, as well as the behavioral patterns that emerge when the decision-maker is directly in control of the SAT. Finally, we discuss the applications of these techniques to the study of decision-making.

Finally, we discuss the applications of these techniques to the study of decision-making.
